real_woman.name: Hedy Lamarr
real_woman.achievement: Invented frequency-hopping technology that became the basis for WiFi, Bluetooth, and GPS

story.title: The Secret Inventor

pages.text: Lily loved Saturdays because Saturday meant the library's Technology Club. She bounced through the double doors with her backpack full of ideas for building a robot out of cardboard and blinking lights. But when she got to the sign-up table, a boy named Marcus looked up and said, "This club is mostly for boys, you know. Boys are better at tech stuff." Two other boys behind him nodded like it was just a fact, like saying the sky is blue. Lily looked around the room and noticed he was sort of right — almost everyone there was a boy. She felt a small, cold stone settle in her stomach. Maybe she should go to the art room instead. She set down the sign-up pen and stepped back from the table.
pages.illustration_description: A library activity room with a large table covered in wires, small motors, and colorful electronic components. A sign reads 'Saturday Technology Club' in bright block letters. Lily stands near the sign-up table holding a backpack, looking uncertain. A boy sits behind the table with a clipboard, and two other boys stand behind him. The room is filled with mostly boys working on projects. The mood is slightly deflating — warm library light but Lily's posture shows hesitation. Soft golden tones in the library contrast with Lily's uncertain expression.

pages.text: Lily wandered down the library's quiet back hallway, the one with the old portraits on the walls and the funny smell of very old books. She pulled out her phone to text her mom to pick her up early. She stared at the little glowing screen and the WiFi symbol in the corner — those curving lines that meant she was connected to the whole world. And that's when something strange happened. A painting on the wall seemed to shimmer, just for a moment, like light on water. It was a portrait of a woman with dark hair and bright, curious eyes. Underneath it, a small golden plaque read: "Hedy Lamarr — Actress and Inventor." Lily tilted her head. An actress AND an inventor? That didn't fit anything she'd been told. She stepped closer, and the woman in the painting seemed to smile.
pages.illustration_description: A quiet, warmly lit library hallway with old wooden frames on the walls. Lily stands holding a phone, the WiFi symbol visible on its screen, looking up at a large portrait on the wall. The portrait shows an elegant woman from the 1940s with dark wavy hair, intelligent eyes, and a knowing smile. The portrait has a subtle magical shimmer around its golden frame — tiny sparkles of light like fireflies. A small golden plaque is visible beneath. The hallway has a sense of mystery and wonder — deep warm colors, amber light from old wall sconces, and dust motes floating in the air like tiny stars.

pages.text: The shimmer around the painting grew brighter, and suddenly the hallway melted away like a dream. Lily found herself standing in a cozy 1940s kitchen, black-and-white checkered floor beneath her feet. A woman sat at a small wooden table covered in papers, pencils, and strange diagrams that looked like secret codes. It was the woman from the portrait — Hedy Lamarr, alive and real, drawing blueprints by lamplight. "Everyone thinks I'm just an actress," Hedy said with a wink, as if she'd been expecting Lily all along. "By day, I was the most famous actress in the world. But by night, I sat right here at my kitchen table, drawing blueprints for an invention that would one day connect billions of people." Lily's mouth fell open. She looked down at the diagrams and saw words like "frequency" and "signal" and little waves drawn in pencil. "You mean... YOU invented something?" Lily whispered. Hedy laughed, a warm and sparkling sound. "Oh, my dear, I invented something extraordinary."
pages.illustration_description: A magical, warmly glowing 1940s kitchen with a black-and-white checkered floor. Hedy Lamarr sits at a small wooden kitchen table under a cone of golden lamplight, surrounded by scattered papers covered in technical drawings, wave diagrams, and mathematical notations. She wears an elegant but simple dress and looks up with a warm, conspiratorial smile. Lily stands across the table, eyes wide with amazement. The scene has a magical quality — the edges of the room are slightly blurred and sparkly, as if this is a vision or dream. The mood is intimate, warm, and enchanting. Pencils, a ruler, and a coffee cup sit on the table among the blueprints.

pages.text: Hedy leaned forward, and the kitchen seemed to glow even brighter as she told her story. "While everyone only saw a beautiful actress, I was secretly inventing technology that would become WiFi and Bluetooth — the invisible magic that connects your phone, your tablet, and your computer to the whole wide world." She explained how she had an idea about making radio signals jump between different frequencies, like a dancer leaping between stages, so that no one could block or steal the message. People laughed at her. "What does a movie star know about science?" they said. But Hedy didn't stop. She studied, she drew, she calculated, and she patented her invention. For years, nobody gave her credit. "They put my face on movie posters," Hedy said softly, "but they hid my blueprints in a drawer." Then, many years later, scientists realized her idea was brilliant. It became the foundation for the technology that connects billions of people every single day. Hedy looked straight at Lily. "Never let anyone tell you what your mind can or cannot do."
pages.illustration_description: A magical, fairy-tale-like spread showing Hedy Lamarr's story in swirling vignettes around the central kitchen scene. In one swirl, Hedy is glamorous on a movie set with bright lights. In another, she sits alone at her kitchen table drawing by lamplight. A visual metaphor shows radio signals dancing like colorful ribbons leaping between musical notes or stages. In a darker swirl, shadowy figures laugh and dismiss her, pushing papers into a drawer. In the brightest swirl at the top, her invention radiates outward like a golden sun, connecting images of modern phones, tablets, laptops, and satellites with glowing lines. Lily watches all of this unfold with wonder. The overall palette moves from dark dismissive tones to brilliant, triumphant gold and electric blue. The mood is epic and awe-inspiring.

pages.text: The kitchen faded like morning mist, and Lily was back in the library hallway, standing in front of the portrait. But everything felt different now. She looked at her phone again — at that little WiFi symbol — and thought: a woman made this possible. Lily put her phone in her pocket and walked straight back to the Technology Club table. She picked up the pen and wrote her name in big, bold letters. Marcus looked surprised. "I thought you left," he said. "I came back," said Lily, pulling her cardboard robot plans from her backpack. "Did you know a woman invented the technology behind WiFi? Her name was Hedy Lamarr, and she was also a movie star." Marcus blinked. "Really?" "Really," said Lily, already reaching for the blinking lights and wires. And as she started to build, she felt that cold little stone in her stomach turn into something warm and bright — like a signal, leaping from frequency to frequency, unstoppable and free.
pages.illustration_description: Back in the bright library activity room, Lily stands confidently at the Technology Club table, writing her name on the sign-up sheet in big letters. Her backpack is open, cardboard robot plans visible inside. Marcus and the other boys look at her with genuine surprise and curiosity. On the table in front of Lily are colorful wires, blinking LED lights, and craft supplies. Lily's posture is tall and confident, with a warm smile. In the background, the hallway is visible, and the portrait of Hedy Lamarr can be seen distantly on the wall, glowing faintly with a golden light. The mood is bright, triumphant, and joyful — warm sunshine streams through library windows. The color palette is vibrant and hopeful — golden light, bright primary colors on the tech supplies, and a sense of energy and possibility.

story.real_woman_achievement: Hedy Lamarr invented frequency-hopping spread spectrum technology during World War II, which became the foundation for modern WiFi, Bluetooth, and GPS — the invisible technology that connects billions of people around the world every day.
discussion_prompts: Has anyone ever told you that something wasn't for you — like a toy, a game, or an activity — just because of who you are? How did that make you feel?
Why do you think people didn't believe Hedy Lamarr could be both a movie star and an inventor? Do you think that was fair?
What is something YOU would like to invent or build, and what would it do to help people?
story.activity_suggestion: Build a 'Secret Inventor Station' at home! Gather cardboard, tape, markers, aluminum foil, and any safe household items. Challenge your child to design and draw blueprints for their own invention — just like Hedy did at her kitchen table. Then try to build a prototype! Talk about what problem the invention solves, and display the blueprints proudly on the wall with a golden plaque that reads their name and the word 'Inventor.'

issues: Marcus and the boys at the table are portrayed somewhat one-dimensionally as gatekeepers, though this is softened by Marcus showing curiosity at the end
The phrase 'beautiful actress' on page 4 slightly emphasizes appearance, though it's contextualized as what 'everyone only saw'
strengths: Lily is described primarily through her actions, interests, and emotions rather than appearance
Hedy Lamarr's story is woven naturally into the narrative through the magical realism device
The story acknowledges the real barrier (mostly boys in the room) without making it the girl's fault or flaw
Language is age-appropriate and avoids being preachy—the moral emerges through story rather than lecture
The ending is empowering but realistic: Lily doesn't suddenly become the best, she just participates with confidence
Excellent use of specific technical details (frequency hopping, patents) that respect young readers' intelligence
The WiFi symbol serves as a powerful recurring motif that connects past to present
Hedy is portrayed with full agency—she studied, persisted despite ridicule, and succeeded
The emotional arc (cold stone to warm brightness) is concrete and relatable
